I'm a bit iffy about passing all these arguments to the class's `__init__`. You could imagine that you can access the same device/ip with different communities, given existing authorisation rules. For example you might use one community for read access, and another for write access.

I think that, if doing something like this, either the `ip` should be moved into `__init__` as well, or, that all arguments except `timeout` should be pushed down to the individual functions again.

Both approaches make sense. It's just that the user experience will be different. In one case you have one `SnmpClient` instance per connection, in the other case you have one SnmpClient instance per application.

I personally prefer the second option. I think it's weird to instantiate a new class every time you want to make a  call.

I think the proposal to simply pass a `Transport` instance into the `SnmpClient` class and leave everything else as-is, might come out the cleanest.

These variable names are incorrect. The definition is `socket.setsockopt(level, optname, value)`, shadowing the underlying C definition (see http://linux.die.net/man/2/setsockopt). More appropriate names would be `level` and `option`. Since we're rewriting this method, it's a good opportunity to clean up this legacy fault, especially when it's this easy.

The names `family` and `type` are also poor choices for the following reason: `family` clobbers the local variable `family` declared above, so that if we wish to refer to that family below (e.g., in logging), we can't, because it's been clobbered; `type` is a bad name, because it's bad practice to override builtins.

`hdfs3` will read from the configuration files the HA settings (see [docs]), but if we're getting the configuration from the connection, we need to at least specify how to do so in the extra section.

I was thinking that we could specify a `ha` key in the extra section, so that extra looks like

```python
{
  'ha': {
    host = "nameservice1"
    conf = {
        "dfs.nameservices": "nameservice1",
        "dfs.ha.namenodes.nameservice1": "namenode113,namenode188",
        "dfs.namenode.rpc-address.nameservice1.namenode113": "hostname_of_server1:8020",
        "dfs.namenode.rpc-address.nameservice1.namenode188": "hostname_of_server2:8020",
        "dfs.namenode.http-address.nameservice1.namenode188": "hostname_of_server1:50070",
        "dfs.namenode.http-address.nameservice1.namenode188": "hostname_of_server2:50070"
    }
} # number of braces might be wrong here :)
```

and then in the code we could do 

```python
ha = params.extra_dejson.get('ha', {})
if ha:
    pars.update(ha.get('conf'))

...
self._conn = hdfs3.HDFileSystem(
    host=params.host or ha.get('host') or MyNone
...
)
```

What do you think?

[docs]: https://hdfs3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/hdfs.html?highlight=kerberos#high-availability-mode

I hear you -- here's the full details:

1. The function is being executed in the cluster, so if we proceed immediately it might not have time to complete
2. We could check the future in a `while` loop until it completes, but we'd have to wrap that in a timeout just in case there is an error communicating with the cluster
3. Therefore, my thought was to skip the while loop and just block for a moment to see if the future completes.

I noticed interesting behavior when `.result()` is not called.  We still block until the download finishes, but if you Ctrl-C the process, we don't have any hooks to run the cleanup.  So for example, you can leave temp files hanging around with something like this:

```
import boto3
import time
from s3transfer.manager import TransferManager

s3 = boto3.client('s3')
m = TransferManager(s3)
r = m.download('bucket', 'large', '/tmp/largefile')

print "Sleeping..."
time.sleep(3)
print "Hit ctrl-c now"
```

You'll see a `/tmp/largefile.*` file still hanging around.  What options (if any) do we have for handling this?

By default, pytest-mock will use the `mock` module that comes with the stdlib in Python 3. However, as the comment explains, in 3.3 the `assert_called_with` method is not agnostic when checking for positional vs. keyword arguments, which was causing our `assert_requested` method to fail for some tests depending on how `api_requestor.request` was called.

To fix this, I added the `mock` pypi module to setup.py for Python<=3.3 and the configuration line above.

I'd rather drop support for 3.3 entirely though and get rid of this workaround.

Do you know why your PRs always include all these white space changes?  It would be helpful if you could somehow avoid committing these changes.  It makes it hard to to the review when I have to verify that most of these line changes just involve a single space at the end of a line being removed, which isn't relevant to anything.

You can use `git add -p` to pick through the local changes and select only a subset of them to commit.

Also, if you want to make the white space changes, you can commit them on master and then merge them into the issue branch, so they won't show up on the pull review.  White space changes like this don't have to be reviewed..  :)

In the limit of nu->infinity, the Spergel profile becomes a Gaussian.  That being the case, I don't think we need to have such a low upper limit for nu.  The practical limit is somewhere around nu = 150 or so, where xnorm start to approach 1.e323 where double precision will overflow.  So perhaps setting nu max to 100 would be reasonable.  I'm sure no one will consider this limit oppressive.  :)

> the main thing I don't understand is how translators are supposed to fully understand the context of what they're translating. 

Oh, I'm so glad somebody said this... :heart_eyes: 
So many times it's a guessing game, and it often makes the job of correctly localizing UI really challenging. 
> Would be optimal if somehow they could see a screenshot of the page with the text highlighted

That would be a big help, but not many CAT tools offer that option, and Crowdin certainly does not, afaik. This is what the translator sees (old `json` file for Management page, just two strings):

![management_module-messages json - kolibri - crowdin translation](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1457929/22159529/a87320ba-df41-11e6-8b23-035f9c0c0423.png)

So `$trNameSpace` and `$tr` are indeed the only context available, and I guess we'll have to find a balance between verbosity and adequate amount of information. Maybe Devon's previous suggestion went a bit over the top, but we can start making a small list of conventions for the most common UI elements as the first part of the `trs`. We would document them in the dev docs and keep updating it as we integrate new features. Majority of translators would have no problem with acronyms like:

- placeholder-msg
- btn-lbl
- dropdown-sel-lbl
- dropdown-sel-opt

```
$trs: {
  'placeholder-msg.noUser': 'No users exist.',
  'placeholder-msg.allUsersFiltered': 'No users match the filter.',
  'btn-lbl.editUser': 'Edit account information',
  'dropdown-sel-lbl.filterUserType': 'Filter User Type',
  'dropdown-sel-opt.admins': 'Admins',
```

Hi @sasha0, thanks for your feedback, understandable considering the way the rest of oscar's tests are implemented. However,  I think updating the fixtures is much, much safer because they are actually made with the user interface, which means they are a real representation of data users produce. It would be much, much more difficult to update the factories to reflect real offers, especially after changes to the offer machinery (models included). For example there is a field called 'exclusive' which was recently added. Without understanding what that field is for, factory boy would just put some random value or the default in there. This could lead to testing of non real world scenarios. I know fixtures are frowned upon by many people, but they are explicit and they are explicitly updated when models change and I think that is a good thing instead of a burden, See zen of python.

I agree with you that `decode` and `encode` could have been designed better. I just think they are very common when writing Python 3 programs and people with experience dealing with I/O in Python 3 will have learned to use these methods before while everyone who works on that part of Certbot would have to learn about our custom methods.

Again, I'm not against you adding these utility methods, I just think always using them when we know the type is a bad idea.

As for that specific issue, I think it luckily doesn't really affect us because our code has to work in Python 2 and 3 where there is no `unicode` type and `str` and `bytes` only have `encode` and `decode` respectively. By having to make our code work in both, I think we avoid the situation of calling `decode` on a `unicode` object or `encode` on a `str` because the equivalent types in Python 3 (`str` and `bytes` respectively) don't have those methods.

this is meant to work for blocksz = any power of 2. The only way to simplify is to assume some specific alignment and substitute the constants. Let's assume 64 byte alignment.  The first term on the rhs, tips the pointer over the closest aligned memory position. The second term is the negation of the power of 2 alignment minus 1. So it leaves all bits on except the bits that correspond to the bit-width of the alignment (in this case : 6 least significant bits will be zero). Doing a binary AND of the first term with the second term zeros out 6 least significant bits, thus causing the result to be 64-byte aligned to the next 64-byte aligned memory position. If it's already aligned, it stays the same.
